#Old title: Arrivals and Exits: A multi-level regression model to analyze visual surveys of spawning salmon
---
title: "Estimating spawning salmon abundance from visual surveys: a hierarchical model of arrival and exit dynamics"
author:
  - name: Scott A. Akenhead 
    email: scott@s4s.com 
    affiliations: 
        - id: pbs 
          name: Pacific Biological Station
          department: Fisheries and Oceans Canada
          address: 3190 Hammond Bay Road
          city: Nanaimo
          state: BC, Canada
          postal-code: V9T 6N7
    note: (retired).
  - name: Colette Louie
    email: CLouie@oib.ca
    affiliations:
        - id: OIB
          name: Osooyos Indian Band
          address: 1155 Sen Pok Chin Blvd
          city: Oliver
          state: BC, Canada
          postal-code: V0H 1T3
  - name: Patrick L. Thompson
    email: patrick.thompson@dfo-mpo.gc.ca
    affiliations:
        - id: psec
          name: Pacific Science Enterprise Center
          department: Fisheries and Oceans Canada
          address: 4160 Marine Dr
          city: West Vancouver
          state: BC, Canada
          postal-code: V7V 1H2
    attributes:
        corresponding: true
date: last-modified
bibliography: bibliography.bib
abstract: |
  The abundance of spawning salmon is a fundamental variable for salmon management, but conventional methods do not estimate the precision of abundance and risk bias from an assumed residence time (to correct for observing a fish in repeated surveys). Here we present a new model that estimates spawner abundance by modelling the process and timing of spawner arrivals and exits from the spawning ground. This model estimates spawner residence time directly from spawner surveys, removing a critical assumption from traditional methods. We first demonstrate how the model is able to accurately estimate parameters from simulated data. Then we demonstrate its application in estimating 25 years of spawner abundance of wild sockeye salmon (_Oncorhynchus nerka_) in the Okanagan River (BC, Canada). Our median estimated residence time of 10.3 days is similar to the 11 days previously assumed for this population. Thus, our total estimated spawner abundances are similar to estimates using the trapezoidal area under the curve with an assumed residence time. Our model analyses all surveys simultaneously using a hierarchical framework, providing annual estimates of spawner abundance, arrival timing and spread, exit lag, and exit spread with associated uncertainties. By directly estimating residence time and integrating uncertainty into abundance estimates, our approach improves the accuracy, transparency, and reliability of spawner abundance estimates. 

keywords: 
  - salmon
  - Oncorhynchus
  - spawner
  - spawner survey
  - Osoyoos Lake
  - Okanagan River
  - Columbia River 

format:
  elsevier-pdf:
    keep-tex: false
    number-sections: true
    journal:
      name: Fisheries Research
      formatting: preprint
      # model: 3p # Don't set a model with preprint
      cite-style: authoryear
appendix: true
execute: 
  cache: true
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library(knitr); 
library(magrittr)
library(tidyverse)
library(tidybayes)
library(rstan)
library(loo)
library(patchwork)
library(ggrepel)
library(janitor)
library(scales)
library(geomtextpath)
library(lubridate)

theme_set(theme_bw())
```

```{r load_data, include=FALSE}
getwd()
file.exists("../data/spawn_timing.csv")

spawners <- read.csv("../data/spawn_timing.csv") %>% 
  mutate(date = as.Date(date), year = year(date), yday = yday(date))

index_sk <- spawners %>% 
  mutate(duplicate = duplicated(.)) %>% 
  filter(duplicate==FALSE) %>% 
  group_by(location, year, yday, date) %>% 
  filter(location == "Index") %>% 
  filter(species == "sk") %>% 
  summarise(
    live = if (all(is.na(live))) NA_real_ else sum(live, na.rm = TRUE),
    dead = if (all(is.na(dead))) NA_real_ else sum(dead, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  arrange(date) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  mutate(day_ind = as.numeric(factor(yday)), 
         year_ind = as.numeric(factor(year))) %>% 
  filter(!is.na(live)) 

index_sk %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = yday, y = 1e-3*live, color = location))+
  geom_point(size=0.5, color='black')+
  facet_wrap(~year,scales="free_y") + 
  theme(strip.background = element_rect(color="NA", fill="NA"))+
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "none")+
  labs(y="Live Spawners Present ('000)", x="Day of the Year")
```

```{r trad_AUC, include = FALSE}
trad_AUC <- index_sk %>%            
  group_by(year) %>%   
  mutate(tdiff = date - lag(date),                      
         tdiff = replace(tdiff, which(tdiff < 0), NA),
         xbar = (live + lag(live))/2, 
         fishdays = case_when(                          
           is.na(xbar) ~ live * 11/2,         
           !is.na(xbar) ~ as.numeric(tdiff) * xbar
         ),
         cumulative = cumsum(fishdays)) %>%  
  group_by(year) %>%     
  summarise(total_auc = max(cumulative, na.rm = TRUE) +
              (live[which.max(date)]*(11/2)), #this adds in the right tail.
            nerkids = round(total_auc/11,0))
```

```{r stan, include = FALSE}
day_stand <- min(index_sk$yday)

priors <- data.frame(prior = c("log_runs_mu", "arrival_mu", "arrival_sigma", "spread","spread_sigma","residence", "count_dispersion"),
                     v1 = c(10,280-day_stand, 0,log(8-1), 0,log(11-1), 1), 
                     v2 = c(1,2,1,0.2, 10, 0.3, 0.2))


sp_dat = list(n_priors = nrow(priors), 
              priors = data.matrix(priors[,-1]),
              n_years = max(index_sk$year_ind),
              year = as.numeric(factor(index_sk$year)),
              day = index_sk$yday-day_stand,
              n_obs = nrow(index_sk),
              live_counts = index_sk$live)

init_fun <- function() list(
      log_run = rnorm(sp_dat$n_years, 10, 1),
      arrival_mu = rnorm(1, 40, 2),
      arrival_sigma = rexp(1, 1), 
      arrival_spread_mu = rnorm(1, log(7), 0.2),
      exit_spread_mu = rnorm(1, log(7), 0.2),
      exit_lag_mu = rnorm(1, log(10), 0.3)
    )


run_or_load_stan <- function(model_name, stan_path, data_list, chains = 4, cores = 4) {
  fit_file <- paste0("../model_fits/", model_name, ".RData")
  hash_file <- paste0("../model_fits/", model_name, ".md5")
  current_hash <- unname(tools::md5sum(stan_path))
  
  if (!file.exists(fit_file) || !file.exists(hash_file) || readLines(hash_file) != current_hash) {
    message("Fitting ", model_name, "...")
    m <- stan_model(file = stan_path)
    fit <- sampling(m, data = data_list, chains = chains, cores = cores, control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95), init = replicate(4, init_fun(), simplify = FALSE))
    save(fit, m, file = fit_file)
    writeLines(current_hash, hash_file)
  } else {
    message("Loading saved fit for ", model_name, "...")
    load(fit_file)
  }
  return(fit)
}

if(!file.exists("../stan/spawners_m1.stan")) stop('STOP no file ../stan/spawners_m1.stan')
if(!dir.exists("../model_fits")) stop('STOP no folder ../model_fits')



fit_m1 <- run_or_load_stan("spawner_m1", "../stan/spawners_m1.stan", sp_dat)
fit_m2 <- run_or_load_stan("spawner_m2", "../stan/spawners_m2.stan", sp_dat)
fit_m3 <- run_or_load_stan("spawner_m3", "../stan/spawners_m3.stan", sp_dat)
fit_m4 <- run_or_load_stan("spawner_m4", "../stan/spawners_m4.stan", sp_dat)
fit_m5 <- run_or_load_stan("spawner_m5", "../stan/spawners_m5.stan", sp_dat)
fit_m6 <- run_or_load_stan("spawner_m6", "../stan/spawners_m6.stan", sp_dat)
fit_m7 <- run_or_load_stan("spawner_m7", "../stan/spawners_m7.stan", sp_dat)
fit_m8 <- run_or_load_stan("spawner_m8", "../stan/spawners_m8.stan", sp_dat)

# model checks
worst_Rhat <- summary(fit_m8)$summary %>% 
  as.data.frame() %>% 
  mutate(Rhat = round(Rhat, 3)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(Rhat))

worst_Rhat %>% 
  filter(n_eff>3) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = n_eff, y = Rhat))+
  geom_point()+
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1.01, lty = 2)+
  geom_vline(xintercept = 400, lty = 2)

loo_m1 <- loo(fit_m1)
loo_m2 <- loo(fit_m2)
loo_m3 <- loo(fit_m3)
loo_m4 <- loo(fit_m4)
loo_m5 <- loo(fit_m5)
loo_m6 <- loo(fit_m6)
loo_m7 <- loo(fit_m7)
loo_m8 <- loo(fit_m8)

loos <- list(
  m1 = loo_m1,
  m2 = loo_m2,
  m3 = loo_m3,
  m4 = loo_m4,
  m5 = loo_m5,
  m6 = loo_m6,
  m7 = loo_m7,
  m8 = loo_m8
)

cmp <- loo_compare(loos)

loo_df <- as.data.frame(cmp) %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("model") %>%
  rename(
    dloo = elpd_diff,
    se = se_diff
  )

fit <- fit_m8
post <- rstan::extract(fit)
```

# Introduction

## The importance of spawner surveys

Spawner abundance is a key variable in salmon ecology. Functions relating spawners to their offspring that return as adults ("stock" and "recruits") are fundamental for management of salmon fisheries and salmon habitats [@ricker1975; @hilborn1992]. For salmon, spawner abundance in a river, stream, or lake beach is typically estimated through repeated visual surveys---for example, from a boat, a helicopter, a drone, or while snorkeling, or walking---that count the number of spawners present across multiple days in the spawning period. However, converting these repeat survey counts into an estimate of total spawner abundance requires accounting for two factors: 1) surveys typically do not cover every day of the spawning period, and 2) spawning fish generally remain on the spawning grounds for multiple days [e.g., 8.8-187 days for sockeye salmon\; @perrin1990]. Conventional methods adjust for these factors but rely on strong assumptions that can introduce bias and fail to account for uncertainty.

The classic, and still widely used method, is based on *Trapezoidal area under the curve* [*TAUC*\; @ames1977; @perrin1990]. This is a non-parametric approach, where "curve" means linear interpolations of counts between survey dates ([a Reimann sum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann_sum "wikipedia")) to fill in missing survey days [@english1992; @irvine1992]. Prior knowledge about first and last dates for presence of spawners is required, and are often assumed. The resulting integral has units of $fish \times days$. To account for repeat observations of individual fish, total spawner abundance is calculated by dividing $fish \times days$ by the average **residence** time---also known as survey life---that a spawner spends on the spawning ground. Residence times have been found to vary across years and across spawning grounds and so it is recommended that annual, spawning-ground specific estimates should be used [@bocking1988; @perrin1990; @irvine1992]. There are a range of methods for estimating residence time [reviewed in @perrin1990]. However, because estimating residence time is difficult and requires additional surveying, residence time is often assumed, sometimes based on similarity between populations [e.g., @hyatt2010]. But, because residence time acts as a scalar on the survey counts, it can introduce substantial bias to the resulting total spawner abundance estimates if the assumed value is incorrect. In addition, TAUC approaches do not account for uncertainty due to observation error, missing data, or residence time in their estimates.

To account for observation error, a continuous curve (e.g., Gaussian or gamma) can be fit to the time series of survey estimates in a year using linear regression [@millar2012]. This produces a *Gaussian area under curve* (*GAUC*), where total spawning abundance can be calculated from the integral of the curve, corrected for residence time as in the TAUC. This approach has been extended by applying hierarchical regression to analyze multiple years or spawning grounds simultaneously, enabling more reliable estimates of spawner abundance when data is sparse by leveraging information shared across years or locations (citations?). While GAUC improves upon TAUC by accounting for uncertainty in the spawner counts, it still relies on an assumed or measured residence time. Additionally, it assumes that the arrival and departure processes---due to death for salmon because the are semelparous---combine together to produce a Gaussian distribution of spawner abundance on the spawning grounds.

Models that mechanistically model the arrival-death processes [*ADP*\; @szerlong2008] address some of the limitations of the TAUC and GAUC approaches. These models assume that arrivals and departures follow the same distribution (e.g., a cumulative normal distribution), lagged by residence. Spawner abundance is then arrivals cumulative to a survey date, minus exits cumulative to that date (@fig-enterexit) [@hilborn1999; @su2001; @szerlong2008]. Residence is then an estimated parameter rather than an assumption [@adkison2001; @labelle2021; @su2001]. Still, by assuming that arrivals and departures follow the same distribution, this approach assumes that residence is the same regardless of whether or not spawners arrive early or late. Evidence from a number of populations across different salmon species suggests this is unlikely; early arriving spawners tend to reside longer that late arriving spawners (citations).

Here we introduce a model that extends existing arrival-death approaches by allowing for greater flexibility in the arrival and departure processes. Instead of a single arrival and departure distribution that is fixed across years, we estimate the average day that spawners arrive on the spawning grounds (**arrival timing**), the variation in their arrival (**arrival spread**), the lag between the average arrival date and the average exit date (**exit lag**), as well as the variation in exit timing (**exit spread**). By decoupling arrival and exit variation, the model allows residence time to depend on arrival order. We fit the model using a hierarchical Bayesian framework, enabling estimation of year-to-year variation in both spawner timing and abundance. This hierarchical structure shares information across years, improving estimates when data is sparse while reducing the risk of overfitting. We first demonstrate the robustness of the model by showing how it can accurately estimate spawner timing and abundance from simulated data. Then we apply it to 25 years of spawner enumeration surveys in the Okanagan River, to illustrate its utility for ecological inference and management-relevant estimation of spawner abundance.

<!-- This is a robust estimator @english1992 that accommodates waves of spawners and asymmetric distributions of spawner abundance with time. Efforts have been made to estimate the precision of TAUC from precision of observations [@askey2023; @korman2002; @parken2003; @millar2013; @parsons2010]. -->

<!-- Estimates of the number of sockeye salmon (*Oncorhynchus nerka*) spawners in a river or a lake beach involve repeated surveys during the spawning period. Methods vary; conventionally by visual counts while boating, snorkeling, walking, or flying in a helicopter or small airplane; and recently analyzing video from drones. There are many other methods to estimate spawner abundance: mark-recapture, conductivity sensors, counting fences, eDNA, and these methods are frequently combined [@parsons2010]. Complications include distinguishing hatchery fish from wild, and separating anadromous Sockeye from non-anadromous Kokanee (sub-species of *O. nerka*). -->

<!-- The conventional analysis of a survey of sockeye spawners determines **AUC**, the integral of spawner count over a number of surveys). Using an assumed value for residence time changes the absolute number of spawners in each survey to an index of relative abundance @askey2023. There are three approaches to estimating the abundances of sockeye spawners from visuals surveys\> AUC: -->

<!-- 1.  Trapezoidal area under curve, **TAUC**. This is the conventional, non-parametric approach, where "curve" means linear interpolations of counts between survey dates (a [Reimann sum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann_sum "wikipedia")) [@english1992; @irvine1992;]. The resulting integral has units $fish \times days$. Prior knowledge about first and last dates for presence of spawners is required, these bounding zeros are often assumed dates. Spawners have a **residence** time on spawning grounds (aka survey life), so a spawner will be counted more than once in a series of surveys. The estimate of **run** size (spawner abundance) is then AUC divided by residence to correct for repeated observations. The estimate for run size is then proportional to 1/residence. This is a robust estimator @english1992 that accomodates waves of spawners and asymmetric distributions of spawner abundance with time. Efforts have been made to estimate the precision of TAUC from precision of observations [@askey2023; @korman2002; @parken2003; @millar2013; @parsons2010]. -->

<!-- 2.  Gaussian area under curve, **GAUC.** A continuous curve, such as the Gaussian or gamma PDD (probability density distribution), is fitted by non-linear regression to the time series of survey estimates in a year @millar2012 . Run size is the integral of that curve, corrected by residence as preceding. This has been extended by multi-level regression to analyze multiple years simultaneously with the advantage of better estimates from poorly observed years. -->

<!-- 3.  Arrival-death process, **ADP.** These are a suite of mechanistic models that reflect the behaviour of spawners [the term is from @szerlong2008]. Timing and spread of arrivals is a PDD, and exits are the same distribution but lagged by residence (Sockeye are semelparous, exits are deaths). Spawner abundance is then arrivals cumulative to a survey date, minus exits cumulative to that date (@fig-enterexit) [@hilborn1999; @su2001; @szerlong2008]. Residence is one of the non-linear, fitted parameters, not an assumption. [@adkison2001; @labelle2021; @su2001]. -->

<!-- Approaches 1 and 2 require an independently estimated value for residence. Methods for this include counts at a spawner fence and/or recapture of fish tagged upon entry [@askey2023; @berghe1986; @cormack1964; @fukushima1997; @korman2002; @lady1998; @liao1994; @manske2000; @neilson1981; @perrin1990; @semenchenko1993]. Estimating, and correcting for, observer efficiency is part of the methods for visual survey. -->

<!-- also @bue1998 and @english1992 and @thomason1984. -->

```{r fig-enterexit, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.height = 4, fig.width = 6, fig.align= 'center', fig.cap = "Example of how the model estimates total spawner abundance, arrival timing, arrival spread, exit lag, and exit spread based on the observed counts (points) of spawners on multiple days. The number of spawners present on a given day is the difference between accumulated arrivals and accumulated exits. We assumed spawner counts are imperfect observations of the true number, so individual points vary from the estimated presence value. The abundance of spawners is estimated as the total number that arrived. All model parameters are estimated with uncertainty — lines show the posterior median and bands show the 95% credible intervals. In the case of arrived and exited numbers, uncertainty increases with abundance because uncertainty from the timing and spread of arrival or exit is multiplied by uncertainty from abundance."}
j <- 25 # used 2024 as example
arrived <- sapply(15:max(sp_dat$day), FUN = function(x) {
  pnorm(x, post$arrival[,j], post$arrival_spread[,j]) * exp(post$log_run[,j])
}) %>% 
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(draw = row_number()) %>%
  pivot_longer(-draw, names_to = "day", values_to = "spawners") %>%
  mutate(day = as.integer(gsub("V", "", day)) + 14) %>% 
  mutate(type = "arrived")

exited <- sapply(15:max(sp_dat$day), FUN = function(x) {
  pnorm(x, post$arrival[,j] + post$exit_lag[,j], post$exit_spread[,j]) * exp(post$log_run[,j])
}) %>% 
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(draw = row_number()) %>%
  pivot_longer(-draw, names_to = "day", values_to = "spawners") %>%
  mutate(day = as.integer(gsub("V", "", day))+14) %>% 
  mutate(type = "exited")

counts <- sapply(15:max(sp_dat$day), FUN = function(x) {
  entered <- pnorm(x, post$arrival[,j], post$arrival_spread[,j])
  exited <- pnorm(x, post$arrival[,j] + post$exit_lag[,j], post$exit_spread[,j])
  
  exp(post$log_run[,j] + log(entered - (entered * exited)))
}) %>% 
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(draw = row_number()) %>%
  pivot_longer(-draw, names_to = "day", values_to = "spawners") %>%
  mutate(day = as.integer(gsub("V", "", day)) + 14) %>% 
  mutate(type = "present")

example_data <- bind_rows(arrived, exited, counts) %>% 
  group_by(type, day) %>% 
  summarise(l89 = quantile(spawners, probs = 0.065), 
            u89 = quantile(spawners, probs = 0.945),
            spawners = median(spawners))

mean_arrival_day <- median(post$arrival[,j])
median_residence <- median(post$exit_lag[,j])
y_value <- median(pnorm(mean_arrival_day, post$arrival[,j], post$arrival_spread[,j]) * exp(post$log_run[,j]))
x_start <- mean_arrival_day + day_stand
x_end <- mean_arrival_day + median_residence + day_stand

residence_df <- data.frame(
  x = x_start,
  xend = x_end,
  y = y_value,
  yend = y_value,
  label = "residence"
)

ggplot(example_data, aes(x = day + day_stand, y = spawners, group = type))+
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = l89, ymax = u89), color = NA, alpha = 0.2)+
  geom_textline(size = 4, linewidth = 1, aes(label = type), hjust = 0.65, family = "sans") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = ~ format(as.Date(.x, origin = "2023-12-31"), "%b ") %>% 
                       paste0(as.integer(format(as.Date(.x, origin = "2023-12-31"), "%d"))),
                     breaks = as.numeric(as.Date(c("2024-09-15", "2024-10-01", "2024-10-15", "2024-11-01", "2024-11-15")) - as.Date("2023-12-31")), 
                     name = "", expand = c(0, 0))+
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "k"), name = "Spawners (thousands)", expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0)), limits = c(0, 1.3e5), breaks = seq(0, 1.25e5, by = 0.25e5))+
  geom_point(data = index_sk %>% filter(year == 2024), aes(x = yday, y = live, group = NA))+
    #geom_textsegment(data = residence_df, aes(x = x_start, xend = x_end, y = y_value, yend = y_value, label = "residence"), linewidth = 0.5, inherit.aes = FALSE, size = 2.8, family = "sans")+
  annotate("text",
           x = Inf,
           y = max(example_data$spawners),  
           label = "total abundance",
           hjust = 1.03,
           vjust = -0.3,
           size = 4,
           family = "sans")+
  theme_bw()+
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

```

# Methods

## Location

Our analysis was confined to the population of wild Sockeye Salmon that spawn in October and November in the Okanagan River (@fig-map) upstream of Osoyoos Lake. After emerging from spawning gravel, mid-April to mid-May, they spend one or two summers (rarely three) as parr in the North Basin of Osoyoos Lake before an ontological shift (near autumnal equinox) to pre-smolts and subsequent migration the following spring as smolts through the reservoirs (with abundant predators), spillways, and smolt passage tunnels of ten hydroelectric dams to reach the Pacific Ocean via the Columbia River.

After one to three years (rarely more) of marine growth, adults return to the Columbia River in late summer and attempt the transit of ten dams in a 950 km freshwater migration, before entering Osoyoos Lake and subsequently spawning yet further upstream. Ascending the Okanagan River from the Columbia River is increasingly compromised by high river temperatures [@isaak2011; @hyatt2020; @bailey2023; @martins2012; @major1967; @quinn1996].

Spawner estimates for Osoyoos sockeye have historically been based on trapezoidal AUC, with an assumed residence time of 11 days [@hyatt2010]. However, residence time was never assessed for this population directly. Therefore, the selected residence time was based on the estimate for the early Stuart sockeye stock [@perrin1990] because that stock has similar average body size, migration distance, and migratory timing [@hyatt2010].

![The spawning grounds for Sockeye Salmon population that rear as parr in the North Basin of Osoyoos Lake are \~20 to 30 km upstream, in the Okanagan River below McIntyre Dam at the outflow of Vaseux Lake. River flows that affect spawners, eggs, and parr are monitored by Environment Canada (EC) at the site indicated. Map by Braden Judson, Pacific Biological Station.](../figures/Okanagan_to_Osoyoo_Lakes.png){#fig-map fig-align="center" width="5in"}

## Spawner surveys

Spawner enumeration surveys have been conducted in the main sockeye spawning area---also known as the Index section---of the Okanagan river between Osoyoos and Vasseau lakes (@fig-map) since `r min(index_sk$year)`. The Index section consists of the section from Deer park to vertical drop structure 12 (VDS12), which is at the Oliver bridge. All together, surveys have been conducted on `r nrow(index_sk)` days, ranging between `r min(table(index_sk$year))` and `r max(table(index_sk$year))` surveys per year. All surveys occurred between `r format(min(lubridate::make_date(2000, lubridate::month(index_sk$date), lubridate::day(index_sk$date))), "%B %e")` and `r format(max(lubridate::make_date(2000, lubridate::month(index_sk$date), lubridate::day(index_sk$date))), "%B %e")`.

Spawner enumeration survey methods are described in detail in @machin2019. They are conducted on a weekly basis during the initial migration phase, then increased to a three-day interval during the peak spawning period (generally October-November). A three to four person crew performs visual surveys of the Index section from a boat. Two observers focus on counting live sockeye (holding and spawning), one on each side of the middle of the boat, while another one or two observers count dead sockeye and other species (Chinook, kokanee, and rainbow trout) from the front of the boat. In addition, side channels throughout the Index section are walked and fish are enumerated. Since 2012, enumeration of each individual reach within the Index section is available, however, we have used the summed counts across all reaches, because this is the resolution of data that is available from 2000--2011. Limited spawning for the Osoyoos population also occurs in the Okanagan river downstream of the Index section, and in the section between Vaseaux Lake and Okanagan Falls Dam. However, we have excluded these sections from our analysis as including them would require additional complexity in our model to account for potential variation in timing. It would also require that we account for the relative abundances across river sections, that vary through time. This extension is a logical next step, but is not necessary for demonstrating our method, which is the goal of this manuscript.

Although the survey crews count dead sockeye, the dead surveys do not provide a consistent estimate of the accumulation of dead spawners that could be used in our model. Dead fish are enumerated but not marked or removed and so can potentially be counted multiple times across survey dates. However, there is a separate deadpitch crew that collects biosamples multiple times over the course of the spawning period, but at a lower frequency than the live counts are conducted. When doing so, this crew removes the sampled fish. Therefore, the counts of dead fish by the enumeration crew represent accumulated dead spawners, with infrequent removal, that is not easily accounted for in a model.

## Model

<!--# this intro paragraph needs work, overlaps intro to this model in Introduction, and a bit redundant with following. #  -->

The timing and spread of spawners' entry to a spawning ground is reasonably modeled as a Gaussian (normal) probability density distribution (PDD). If residence is constant (ignoring sex, age, size, condition, injury, history, and genetics), then the PDD of spawner exits is the same as the PDD of entry, just delayed. The number of live spawners in a survey day is then the cumulative distribution of entries to that day minus the cumulative distribution of exits (@fig-enterexit). This model is sufficient to estimate runs and residence simultaneously, but assumes all spawners are identical. In what follows, the assumption of fixed residence is relaxed to allow exit spread differ from entry spread.

We modelled spawner abundance based on observed spawner counts using a series of hierarchical Bayesian models fit in the Stan programming language [@stan]. These models varied in complexity and the degree to which they allowed for interannual variation in the arrival and exit processes. However, all models follow the general set of processes shown in @fig-enterexit. Live spawners counts are integers and can include zero. Therefore, we used a Negative Binomial distribution for the observation model:

$$N_{y,d}^{\text{live}} \sim \text{NegBinomial}(\mu^\text{live}_{y,d}, \phi^{\text{live}})$$ {#eq-obs} where $\mu^\text{live}_{y,d}$ is the expected number of live spawners present in year $y$ on day $d$, and $\phi^\text{live}$ captures variability around $\mu^\text{live}_{y,d}$, including both observation error and natural variation that is not explicitly modelled. We assume that this expected number of spawners $\mu^\text{live}_{y,d}$ is a function of three unobserved (i.e., latent) quantities that are estimated in our model: the proportion of spawners that have arrived by day $d$, $p^\text{arrived}_{y,d}$; the proportion of those that have exited the live population, $p^\text{exited}_{y,d}$ (i.e., died or left the spawning grounds), and total spawner abundance in that year, $N_y^{\text{spawners}}$: $$\mu^\text{live}_{y,d} \approx p^\text{arrived}_{y,d} \times (1 - p^\text{exited}_{y,d}) \times N_y^{\text{spawners}}.$$ {#eq-live} We assume that the spawners arrive over a range of days that follows a normal distribution, reside there for some days of spawning activity, and then die or leave the spawning grounds, thereby exiting the live counts following a second normal distribution that lags the arrival distribution. Thus, the proportion of spawners that have arrived by a given date $d$ in year $y$ follows a cumulative normal distribution: $$p^\text{arrived}_{y,d} = \Phi\left( \frac{ d - \mu^\text{arrival}_y }{ \sigma^\text{arrival}_y } \right).$$ {#eq-arrived} Likewise, the proportion of arrived spawners that have exited the live count by a given date $d$ in year $y$ follows a second cumulative normal distribution: $$p^\text{exited}_{y,d} = \Phi\left( \frac{ d - (\mu^\text{arrival}_y + \mu^\text{exit lag}_y)}{\sigma^\text{exit}_y} \right),$$ {#eq-exited} where $\mu^\text{exit lag}_y$ defines the number of days by which the mean exit date lags behind the mean arrival date $\mu^\text{arrival}_y$. Total spawner abundance, $N_y^{\text{spawners}}$, in each year is a primary variable of interest and is estimated by the model as the scaling factor that links the estimated arrival and exit processes to the expected live spawners, $mu^\text{live}_{y,d}$. We set a prior on spawner abundance as $log(N_y^{\text{spawners}}) \sim \text{Normal}$(`r priors[1,2]`, `r priors[1,3]`). This weakly informative prior is consistent with the range of log-transformed spawner abundances previously estimated for this population using trapezoidal AUC, but does not constrain estimates to match those values.

To prevent numerical instability when equation @eq-live approaches zero, we use a log-transformed approximation of the same form: $$\mu^\text{live}_{y,d} =  \exp\left( \log\left(1 + 10^4 \times p^\text{arrived}_{y,d} \times (1 - p^\text{exited}_{y,d})\right) - \log(10^4) \right) \times N_y^{\text{spawners}}$$ {#eq-livefull} where the factor $10^4$ serves as a scaling factor that prevents changes in near-zero values from having an abrupt influence on $\mu^\text{live}_{y,d}$.

In the equations above, we specified $\mu^\text{arrival}_y$, $\sigma^\text{arrival}_y$, $\mu_y^\text{exit lag}$ and $\sigma^\text{exit}_y$ using subscript $y$ to indicate that they vary from year-to-year. However, it is not clear that all of these processes should vary annually, so we compared a series of models with different combinations of varying and constant versions of these parameters (summarised in @fig-modcompare A) and evaluated their fit to withheld data, as described in a subsequent section.

The time-varying versions of these parameters were estimated using a hierarchical structure, where yearly values were drawn from a hyperdistribution (e.g., $\mu^\text{arrival}_y \sim \text{Normal}(\mu^\text{arrival}_\mu, \mu^\text{arrival}_\sigma)$) defined by the multiyear mean and year-to-year standard deviation. We set a prior for the arrival day group mean as $\mu^\text{arrival}_\mu \sim \text{Normal}$(`r priors[2,2]`, `r priors[2,3]`), corresponding to a mean of approximately `r round(priors[2,2]/max(sp_dat$day)*100)` % of the typical spawning survey period, with a prior interval spanning `r round((priors[2,2] - 1.96*priors[2,3])/max(sp_dat$day)*100)`--`r round((priors[2,2] + 1.96*priors[2,3])/max(sp_dat$day)*100)` days. This reflects our expectation that peak arrivals occur during the early to mid portion of the survey window. We used the prior $\mu_\sigma^{\text{arrival}} ~ \text{Exponential}$(`r priors[3,3]`) to represent a moderate level of expected year-to-year variation in $\mu_y^{\text{arrival}}$. We modelled $\sigma^\text{arrival}_y$, $\mu_y^\text{exit lag}$ and $\sigma^\text{exit}_y$ using priors of the form $\text{Exponential}(\lambda)+1$ to ensure that parameters were strictly positive and bounded away from zero following the biological constraint that spawners vary in their arrival and exit timing, and that they typically remain on the spawning grounds for at least one day. We set priors for $\sigma_\mu^\text{arrival}$ and $\sigma_\mu^\text{exit}$ as $\sim \text{Normal}(\log$(`r exp(priors[4,2])`), `r priors[4,3]`), corresponding to an expectation that arrival and exit timing should have similar spread. This corresponds to an expected range of duration between `r round(exp(priors[4,2] - 1.96* priors[4,3])+1)` and `r round(exp(priors[4,2] + 1.96* priors[4,3])+1)` days. We set the prior for $\mu_\mu^\text{exit lag}$ as $\sim \text{Normal}(\log$(`r exp(priors[6,2])`), `r priors[6,3]`), aligning with the previously assumed residence time of approximately 11 days, and with likely values ranging from `r round(exp(priors[6,2] - 1.96* priors[6,3])+1)` to `r round(exp(priors[6,2] + 1.96* priors[6,3])+1)` days. We used the same $\sim \text{Exponential}$(`r priors[5,3]`) prior for $\mu_\sigma^{\text{spread}}, \mu_\sigma^{\text{exit}}$, and $\mu_\sigma^{\text{exit lag}}$ to represent a moderate level of expected year-to-year variation in these parameters on the log-transformed scale.

In the models where we use constant versions of the timing parameters, we drop the subscript $y$ and they are denoted as: $\mu^\text{arrival}$, $\sigma^\text{arrival}$, $\mu^\text{exit lag}$, and $\sigma^\text{exit}$. In these cases, we use the prior for the group level $\mu$ and omit the group-level variance parameter, thus removing the hierarchical structure. The full model equations including priors are presented in @eq-fullmodel.

### Diagnostics

An essential concept in Bayesian statistics is that the data is known but the best model for that data is unknown [@mcelreath2020] and always a vast simplification of reality [@jaynes2003]. Metrics to compare and rank competing models are required. In ecological systems, a superabundance of habitat indicators as covariates means over-fitting is a problem. Leave-one-out (LOO) statistics[^1], where each point is predicted while omitted from fitting (cross-validations), provides diagnostics for over-fitting. We used ELPD, the sum of expected log predictive probability density for each of $n$ omitted observation's prediction, and $\mathrm{ELPD}_{SE}=\text{sd}(\mathrm{ELPD})/\sqrt(n)$ the standard error of those probabilities [@vehtari2017].

[^1]: see R package LOO <https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/loo/loo.pdf> and LOO Package Glossary <https://mc-stan.org/loo/reference/loo-glossary.html>.

## Mean residence

<!--# The parameter exit_lag replaces "residence" as used for AUC, but exit_lag works in conjuction with exit_spread.  The backcalculated value, AUC/run, might be better called "effective residence" to (a) emphasize it is derived, and (b) prevent confusion with "mean of conventional residence values." # -->

Although $\mu^\text{exit lag}_y$ defines the average delay between arrival and exit distributions, it does not equal the mean residence time. This is because residence time depends on the interaction of both arrival and exit processes and is not directly estimated by any single parameter of the model. Importantly, whenever $\sigma^\text{arrival}_y \neq \sigma^\text{exit}_y$, residence time cannot be the same for all individuals, and instead varies depending on when the fish arrive. We can, however, calculate a mean residence time in each year based on the sum of estimated number of live spawners across all days in the spawning period, $D$, divided by the estimated total spawner abundance: $$\mu^{\text{residence}}_y = \frac{\sum_{d = 1}^D \left(p^\text{arrived}_{y,d} \times (1 - p^\text{exited}_{y,d}) \times N_y^{\text{spawners}}\right)}{N_y^{\text{spawners}}}.$$ {#eq-residence} This is the equivalent of back calculating mean residence time from the area under the curve, which is possible because we have directly estimated $N_y^{\text{spawners}}$. However, because $N_y^{\text{spawners}}$ appears in both the numerator and the denominator of @eq-residence, we can simplify this equation as: $$\mu^{\text{residence}}_y = \sum_{d = 1}^D \left(p^\text{arrived}_{y,d} \times (1 - p^\text{exited}_{y,d})\right).$$ {#eq-residence-simple}

## Simulations

<!--# an evolving section! # -->

We challenged this model with simulated observations to ensure robust and reliable results. Parameters were sampled randomly from prior PDDs. Simulated surveys were deliberately poor: the number of surveys each year was random uniform between 6 and 10, and survey dates within years random uniform but separated by at least 1 day. The resulting "true" data, but with multiplicative normal sampling error (% accuracy), was the "observed" data subsequently fitted by the multi-level regression coded in Stan. The scenarios explored fits (see Stan Model) with and without surveys of newly dead spawners. Despite noisy observations and gaps between surveys, parameters were recovered accurately from Stan model, with the caveat that the model used to fit the simulated data was the model that created it. Specifically, this procedure correctly estimated residence over a wide range. The simulations also generated indices of relative abundance of spawners, such as AUC as Gaussian and as linear interpolations (a trapezoid), peak live, and peak live plus dead (PLD). These were compared to the fitted run size to indicate reliability.

# Results

<!--# Simulations recapture residence. What about a summary table of the non-annual parameter estimates, with uncertainty and descriptions. #  -->

## Model selection and convergence

Based on ELPD statistics (@fig-modcompare B) we selected model 8 as the best from those explored. This model was the most complex of those considered and it included year-to-year variation in spawner abundance, arrival timing, arrival spread, and exit spread, and residence time.

The effective number of parameters ($\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{loo}}$) increased when arrival timing variation was introduced (model 1 vs. 2), and also when any of arrival spread, exit spread, or residence were allowed to vary (models 3 to 8; @fig-modcompare C). However, all models with variation in any or all of these parameters (models 3 to 8) had similar numbers of effective parameters.

Convergence was good for model 8; the highest $\hat{R}$ was `r round(max(worst_Rhat$Rhat),3)` and `r round(mean(worst_Rhat$n_eff>400)*100, 1)`% of its parameters with effective sample sizes $>400$, the lowest being `r round(min(worst_Rhat$n_eff))`.

```{r fig-modcompare, echo=FALSE, , warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.height = 5, fig.width = 9, fig.align= 'center', fig.cap= "Model summary and comparison. *Panel A* shows which parameters were held constant (white) versus varied (black) across years in each of the candidate models (rows). *Panel B* shows the relative expected log predictive density ($\\Delta\\mathrm{ELPD}$) compared to the model with the highest out-of-sample predictive accuracy. *Panel C* shows the effective number of parameters ($\\mathrm{p}_\\mathrm{loo}$). The model with the highest out-of-sample predictive accuracy is highlighted in red in panels B and C. Points and error bars (B and C) represent means and standard errors."}

data.frame(model = c("m1","m2","m3","m4", "m5", "m6","m7", "m8"), 
           arrival = c("constant", "varies", "varies", "varies", "varies", "varies", "varies", "varies"), 
           arrival_spread = c("constant", "constant","varies", "constant", "constant", "varies", "varies", "varies"), 
           exit_spread = c("constant", "constant", "constant", "varies", "constant", "constant", "varies", "varies"),
           residence = c("constant", "constant", "constant", "constant", "varies", "varies", "constant", "varies")) %>%
  gather(key = parameter, value = value, -model) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = parameter, y = model, fill = value))+
    geom_tile(color = "grey")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("NA", "black"), "")+
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev)+
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")+
  coord_cartesian(expand = FALSE)+
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank())+
  labs(x = "Parameter", y = "Model")+
  scale_x_discrete(
  labels = c(
    "arrival" = expression(mu[y]^arrival),
    "arrival_spread" = expression(sigma[y]^arrival),
    "exit_spread" = expression(sigma[y]^exit),
    "residence" = expression(mu[y]^"exit lag")
  )
)+
  theme_bw()+
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom")+


ggplot(loo_df, aes(x = model, y = dloo, color = dloo==0)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = dloo - se, ymax = dloo + se), width = 0) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = rev)+
  labs(
    x = "",
    y = expression(Delta*ELPD~"(rel. to best)")
  )+
  theme(panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank())+
  scale_color_manual(values = c(1, "red"), guide = "none")+
  theme_bw()+
  
  ggplot(loo_df, aes(x = model, y = p_loo, color = dloo==0)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = p_loo - se_p_loo, ymax = p_loo  + se_p_loo), width = 0) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = rev)+
  labs(
    x = "",
    y = expression("effective parameters (" * p[loo] * ")")
  )+
  scale_color_manual(values = c(1, "red"), guide = "none")+
  theme_bw()+
  theme(panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank())+

  
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")+
  plot_layout(widths = c(1, 0.5, 0.5))
```

## Model fit to observed counts

Our expected counts correspond well with the observed counts @fig-spawnercurves and @fig-obsfit. Variation between the observed and expected is quantified in our estimate of spawner count overdispersion ($\phi^\mathrm{live}$), which had a posterior median of `r round(median(post$live_phi), 1)` (95% CI: `r round(quantile(post$live_phi, 0.025), 1)`--`r round(quantile(post$live_phi, 0.975), 1)`).

```{=html}
<!--@Scott - you asked for free-y axis on this figure. I tried this, but that really emphasizes the deviations from the predicted for the high count values. This does the dual job of showing the fit and the year to year variability in total abundance, linking it to the abundance time series figure. I don't think the log fit is necessary. But, if you have strong feelings, I am happy to change.
@JLT - reply: IMHO important to reveal nature of observations vs fit: residuals = sampling error + process error. If highest counts are disproportionately variable don't hide that (but this is not a field methods paper). Similarly, log fit reveals nature of lowest observations, and more clearly shows skew of fitted distribution. Skew is when exit_spread differs from arrival_spread, so worth noting. BUT that can be relegated to the simulation in Supplementary Material. -->
```

<!-- which are more precise, small or large counts? which are more important? This is linked to effect of zeros on the model. -->

```{r fig-spawnercurves, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.cap="Observed versus estimated spawners present over the spawning period in each year (panels). Points show the observed counts, the lines and shaded bands show the posterior median the 95% credible intervals. Y-axis limits vary across panels to emphasize model fit rather than variation across years.", fig.align='center', fig.height=7, fig.width=8}
spawn_curves.df <- data.frame()
for(j in 1:ncol(post$log_run)){
  live <- sapply(1:max(sp_dat$day), FUN = function(x) {
    entered <- pnorm(x, post$arrival[,j], post$arrival_spread[,j])
    exited <- pnorm(x, post$arrival[,j] + post$exit_lag[,j], post$exit_spread[,j])
    
    exp(post$log_run[,j] + log(entered - (entered * exited)))
  }
)
  spawn_curves.df <- bind_rows(spawn_curves.df, data.frame(
      year = j + min(spawners$year)-1, 
      day = 1:max(sp_dat$day)+day_stand,
      fish = apply(live, 2, median),
      l95 = apply(live, 2, quantile, probs = 0.025),
      u95 = apply(live, 2, quantile, probs = 0.975)))
}

ggplot(spawn_curves.df, aes(x = day, y = fish))+
  geom_line()+
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = l95, ymax = u95), color = NA, alpha = 0.2)+
  facet_wrap(~year, scales = "free_y")+
  geom_point(data = index_sk, aes(x = yday, y = live), size = 0.8)+
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "k"), name = "Spawners present (thousands)", expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02)))+
  theme_bw()+
  theme(strip.background = element_rect(color="NA", fill="NA"))+
  scale_x_continuous(labels = ~ format(as.Date(.x, origin = "2023-12-31"), "%b"), 
                     breaks = as.numeric(as.Date(c("2024-09-01", "2024-10-01", "2024-11-01")) - as.Date("2023-12-31")), name = "")+
    theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
  
```

```{r effective-residence, include = FALSE}
effective_residence <- matrix(NA, nrow = length(post$arrival_mu), ncol = ncol(post$arrival))
for(j in 1:ncol(post$log_run)){
  live <- sapply(1:max(sp_dat$day), FUN = function(x) {
    entered <- pnorm(x, post$arrival[,j], post$arrival_spread[,j])
    exited <- pnorm(x, post$arrival[,j] + post$exit_lag[,j], post$exit_spread[,j])
    
    entered * (1 - exited)
  })
  
  effective_residence[,j] <- apply(live, 1, sum)
}

colnames(effective_residence) <- unique(index_sk$year)

```

<!-- from @Scott: the confidence/uncertainty intervals for spawning behaviour/timing parameters are the default: .width = c(0.66, 0.95). So bars are 66% and 90%. note +/- 1 stdev is 68.27%.  I would have gone with 50% and 95% CLs: .width = c(0.5, 0.975). -->

## Spawning timing

Arrival timing ($\mu_y^\text{arrival}$) varied across years (@fig-timings A), with posterior medians ranging from `r format(lubridate::ymd("2025-01-01") + round(min(apply(post$arrival, 2, median))+day_stand - 1), "%B %e")` to `r format(lubridate::ymd("2025-01-01") + round(max(apply(post$arrival, 2, median))+day_stand - 1), "%B %e")`. Arrival spread ($\sigma_y^\text{arrival}$) varied across years (@fig-timings B), with posterior medians ranging from `r round(min(apply(post$arrival_spread, 2, median)),1)` to `r round(max(apply(post$arrival_spread, 2, median)),1)` days. Exit lag ($\mu_y^\text{exit lag}$) varied across years (@fig-timings C), with posterior medians ranging from `r round(min(apply(post$exit_lag, 2, median)),1)` to `r round(max(apply(post$exit_lag, 2, median)),1)` days. Exit spread ($\sigma_y^\text{exit}$) tended to be lower than arrival spread (@fig-timings D), with posterior medians ranging from `r round(min(apply(post$exit_spread, 2, median)),1)` to `r round(max(apply(post$exit_spread, 2, median)),1)` days.

Together, the timing parameters result in year-to-year variation in the mean residence time (@fig-timings E). The posterior median across years is `r round(median(effective_residence), 1)` days, but it ranges from `r round(min(apply(effective_residence, 2, median)), 1)` to `r round(max(apply(effective_residence, 2, median)), 1)` days.

```{r fig-timings, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of mean arrival ($\\mu_y^{\\text{arrival}}$; A), arrival spread ($\\sigma_y^{\\text{arrival}}$; B), exit spread ($\\sigma_y^{\\text{exit}}$; C), and the resulting mean residence time ($\\mu^{\\text{residence}}_y$; D). The dashed line in panel D shows the previously assumed 11 day residence time. Points and error bars show the posterior median and the 66% and 95% credible intervals.", fig.align='center', fig.height=9, fig.width=6}
spread_draws(fit, arrival[year]) %>% 
  mutate(year = year + min(spawners$year)-1) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = arrival + day_stand))+
  stat_pointinterval()+
  xlab("")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels = ~ format(as.Date(.x, origin = "2023-12-31"), "%b %d"), 
                     breaks = seq(280, 292, by = 3), name = bquote("Mean arrival (" * mu[y]^"arrival" * ")"))+
  theme_bw()+
  
  spread_draws(fit, arrival_spread[year]) %>% 
  mutate(year = year + min(spawners$year)-1) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = arrival_spread))+
  stat_pointinterval()+
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 20, by = 3))+
  ylab(bquote("Arrival spread (" * sigma[y]^arrival * ")"))+
  xlab("")+
  theme_bw()+
  
  spread_draws(fit, exit_lag[year]) %>% 
  mutate(year = year + min(spawners$year)-1) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = exit_lag))+
  stat_pointinterval()+
  plot_layout(ncol = 1)+
  ylab(bquote("Exit lag (" * mu[y]^exit_lag * ")"))+
  xlab("")+
  theme_bw()+
  
  spread_draws(fit, exit_spread[year]) %>% 
  mutate(year = year + min(spawners$year)-1) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = exit_spread))+
  stat_pointinterval()+
  plot_layout(ncol = 1)+
  ylab(bquote("Exit spread (" * sigma[y]^exit * ")"))+
  xlab("")+
  theme_bw()+
  
  as.data.frame(effective_residence) %>% 
  mutate(draws = row_number()) %>% 
  pivot_longer(-draws, names_to = "year", values_to = "mean_residence") %>% 
  mutate(year = as.numeric(year)) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = mean_residence))+
  stat_pointinterval()+
  geom_hline(yintercept = 11, lty = 2)+
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 30, by = 3))+
  ylab(bquote("Mean residence (" * mu[y]^residence * ")"))+
  theme_bw()+
  
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")
```

## Total spawner abundance

```{r AUCvsmodel, include = FALSE}

run_intervals <- spread_draws(fit, log_run[year]) %>%
  mutate(year = year + min(spawners$year) - 1) %>%
  mutate(run = exp(log_run)) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(lower = quantile(run, 0.17),
            upper = quantile(run, 0.83),
            median = median(run))

AUC_in_66 <- trad_AUC %>%
  left_join(run_intervals, by = "year") %>%
  mutate(in_66 = nerkids > lower & nerkids < upper, 
         lower =  median > nerkids) 

perc_diff <- spread_draws(fit, log_run[year]) %>%
  mutate(year = year + min(spawners$year) - 1) %>%
  mutate(run = exp(log_run)) %>% 
  left_join(trad_AUC) %>% 
  mutate(perc_diff = 100 * (run-nerkids)/((run + nerkids)/2)) %>% 
   group_by(year) %>% 
  summarise(perc_diff = median(perc_diff)) %>% 
  ungroup()
  
```

Estimated posterior median annual spawner abundances ($N_y^\text{spawners}$) varied from as low as `r round(min(apply(exp(post$log_run), 2, median)),-2)` in `r which(apply(exp(post$log_run), 2, median) == min(apply(exp(post$log_run), 2, median))) + min(spawners$year)-1` to as high as `r scales::comma(round(max(apply(exp(post$log_run), 2, median)),-3))` in `r which(apply(exp(post$log_run), 2, median) == max(apply(exp(post$log_run), 2, median))) + min(spawners$year)-1`. Since 2008, spawner abundance has alternated between low and high levels in a two-year cycle.

Our estimated annual abundances are relatively close to those obtained from the trapezoidal AUC estimate with an assumed 11 day residence time, with the traditional estimate falling within our 66% credible interval in `r sum(AUC_in_66$in_66)` out of `r length(AUC_in_66$in_66)` years. Across all years, our model estimates are a median of `r -(round(median(perc_diff$perc_diff), 1))`% lower than those based on TAUC (inter-annual range: `r round(min(perc_diff$perc_diff), 1)`--`r round(max(perc_diff$perc_diff), 1)`%).

```{r fig-runs, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.cap="Posterior distributions of total spawning abundance by year (black) compared to the point estimate based on traditional trapezoidal AUC with an assumed 11 day residence (red). Points and error bars show the posterior median and the 66% and 95% credible intervals.", fig.align='center', fig.height=4, fig.width=7}
spread_draws(fit, log_run[year]) %>% 
  mutate(year = year + min(spawners$year)-1) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = exp(log_run)))+
  stat_pointinterval(aes(color = "modelled"))+
  geom_point(data = trad_AUC, aes(x = year, y = nerkids, color = "trad. AUC"))+
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "k"), name = "Spawner abundance (thousands)", expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0)))+
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 2.5e5))+
  scale_color_manual(values = c(1, "red"), "")+
  xlab("")+
  theme_bw()
```

# Discussion

<!--  General:- new model is successful.Which parameters (models) were important , which not (varying residence? autocorrelation of surveys)? Residence varies little, in this case, so conventional AUC was a reliable relative index. How did Kim get an accurate residence?How will ecologists accommodate biometrics in this model? (like males enter first, big females stay longer,) Scope for covariates: biometrics and habitat, parameter correlations,. -->

Conventional approaches to estimating salmon spawner abundance, such as the TAUC method [@ames1977; @perrin1990], rely on fixed assumptions about residence time and ignore observation error and interannual variability in spawning dynamics. While subsequent methods have addressed some of these limitations individually [e.g., @millar2012; @szerlong2008; @hilborn1999; @su2001], none have fully integrated them in one modelling framework. Building on these previous efforts, our model addresses these gaps by jointly estimating annual run size, arrival timing, arrival spread, exit lag, and exit spread, while accounting for observation error and associated uncertainty. Simulation results demonstrate that the model can recover accurate estimates of the parameters across a range of spawning dynamics. The application to the `r length(unique(index_sk$year))`-year time series of Okanagan sockeye data further illustrates its utility for providing robust abundance estimates that can support sustainable management of the population as well as insight into its ecology. 

## Residence time
Estimating residence time directly from the spawner surveys represents an important advance over conventional methods that rely on an assumed value. Because residence time directly scales the estimated spawner abundance, incorrect assumptions can lead to substantial bias---underestimating abundances if assumed residence is too high, or overestimating if it is assumed too low. Instead of assuming a fixed residence time, our method estimates the arrival and exit processes in each year. This idea is not new; @hilborn1999 demonstrated how residence time could be estimated from visual spawner data. Building on their approach, our method uses a hierarchical structure to estimate interannual variability in residence time, while reducing the risk of overfitting to noisy or sparse data. In our application to Okanagan sockeye, we estimate the median *effective residence* time to be `r round(median(effective_residence), 1)` days, which is very close to the previously assumed 11 days [@hyatt2010]. However, residence time varies across years from `r round(min(apply(effective_residence, 2, median)), 1)` to `r round(max(apply(effective_residence, 2, median)), 1)` days, suggesting that assuming a fixed value likely introduces bias in specific years. 

## Precision
Our method provides uncertainty estimates for spawner abundance and all spawning timing parameters, an important advantage over conventional methods such as TAUC that provide point estimates without uncertainty. The GAUC method [@millar2012] already provides uncertainty estimates that account for observation error and mechanistic arrival-death process models [@szerlong2008; @hilborn1999; @su2001] can propagate uncertainty in residence time. However, the hierarchical Bayesian structure used here enables joint estimation of all parameters and ensures that uncertainty is consistently propagated throughout the model. 

Uncertainty is a key piece of information for fisheries management, enabling decision-makers to assess risk and apply the precautionary approach when managing stocks [@harwood2003]. In addition, uncertainty estimates from spawner abundance models can be propagated to subsequent life-stage models, such as those estimating parr and smolt production or adult returns from spawners. While uncertainty is often viewed as a weakness, ignoring it can result in overconfident abundance estimates, which can obscure true relationships or inflate false signals [@mcelreath2020]. 

## Ecology 
Through modelling the underlying process of spawning arrival and exit, our model produces ecologically informative parameters that provide new insight into the timing of spawning dynamics. These parameters are not available through TAUC or GAUC. While some are estimated in previous arrival-death process models [@szerlong2008; @hilborn1999; @su2001], our model offers greater ecological resolution by allowing separate distributions for arrivals and departures. 

Applying this model in the Okanagan sockeye population, we observe considerable interannual variation in some aspects of spawning timing. Arrival timing was surprisingly consistent, ranging by only `r round(max(apply(post$arrival, 2, median))+day_stand - 1) - round(min(apply(post$arrival, 2, median))+day_stand - 1)` days. In contrast, arrival spread varied considerably from year-to-year, ranging from `r round(min(apply(post$arrival_spread, 2, median)), 1)` days in `r unique(index_sk$year)[which(apply(post$arrival_spread, 2, median) == min(apply(post$arrival_spread, 2, median)))]` to `r round(max(apply(post$arrival_spread, 2, median)),1)` days in `r unique(index_sk$year)[which(apply(post$arrival_spread, 2, median) == max(apply(post$arrival_spread, 2, median)))]`. Exit spread was less variable than arrival spread but showed an overall increasing trend, averaging `r round(mean(apply(post$exit_spread[,1:5], 2, median)),1)` days in the first five years and `r round(mean(apply(post$exit_spread[,(length(unique(index_sk$year))-5):length(unique(index_sk$year))], 2, median)),1)` days in the last five years. Inspection of @fig-timings does not reveal any obvious patterns of covariance between the timing parameters. Although our model does not include covariance structure between timing parameters, incorporating one could improve parameter estimation and potentially reduce uncertainty if such covariation does exist. In Okanagan sockeye, we estimate that exit spread tends to be smaller than arrival spread, which implies that spawner residence time is a function of order of entry (early arrivals stay longer). This pattern has been identified in other studies, where it has been attributed to the fact that fish of different age and sex may arrive at different times (ADD REFERENCES). 

Extending the analysis of Okanagan sockeye to estimate how the spawning timing depends on environmental conditions is an obvious next step. @perrin1990 identified a suite of potential covariates for residence: spawner density, sex, size, sex ratio, mean arrival timing (years that are late may have shorter residence), order of arrival, stream flow, and water temperature. In Atlantic salmon, older and larger spawners arrive earlier than younger and smaller spawners, and female salmon arrive earlier in the season than males [@miettinen2024]. In sockeye, however, males tend to arrive earlier, and remain alive longer than females [@mathisen1962]. Future work could use our model to estimate how biometric and environmental factors influence each of the timing parameters of our model. 

Notably, the widest spread in arrival times occurred in 2010, which may be related to the Testalinden Creek dam failure that occurred the previous spring (June 13, 2010). When this occurred, silt and toxins were discharged into the Okanagan River [@morhart2010; @tannant2013]. The discharge was downstream of the spawning region but affected Osoyoos Lake and the Okanagan River below it. Suboptimal conditions there may have contributed to the increased variation in the timing of spawner arrival that year. Of all the model parameters, only arrival spread appears to be associated with the Testalinden dam failure; how, or if, the event directly affected spawners remains unclear. 

# Observer accuracy
One persistent challenge with spawner enumeration data is the fact that observations are made with error. In many cases, TAUC methods do not account for observation error at all, although methods exist to do this [@parken2011]. Observer efficiency is typically expected to cause a negative bias; that is, observers only see a fraction of the fish. However, overestimation is also possible. A range of factors, including turbidity, cloud cover, observer identity, survey method, and even spawner abundance have the potential to affect the precision of the counts. Thus, a logical extension of our model would be to allow the overdispersion parameter of the negative binomial distribution in our model, $\phi^{\text{live}}$, which captures the variability of the counts around around their expected value, to vary as a function of one or more of these factors.


## Robustness

The duration, consistency, and quality of survey data for Osoyoos Sockeye is exemplary, but how will this model fare with less than ideal data? We examined how robust it is by asking two questions: (1) what is the effect of increasing gaps between the surveys in each year (survey density), and (2) how effective is the information from multiple years at improving estimates from a single year with few surveys. Using simulation models that draw random annual values for run size and the four spawning parameters (see Supplementary Materials) we compared how the bias and precision (components of accuracy) of estimates changed with survey gaps of 1, 3, 5, and 7 days. and estimate of spawner abundance wthe effect of newly dead spawners. The model was robust to gaps in surveys, as long as the gaps were not too large. The model was also robust to the inclusion of newly dead spawners, which improved the precision of estimates of exit lag and residence time. However, the model was not robust to very large gaps in surveys or to very small numbers of newly dead spawners.

<!-- ## Conventional TAUC -->

<!-- We compared our model to TAUC calculated from the surveys. Using the fixed assumption of 11 days for residence time produce small bias as described above. We also compared the variability of TAUC to the model estimates of run size.\ -->
<!-- <!--# Patrick - you have results and discussion for this, right?  --> -->

<!-- We applied the simulation model to examine the bias and precision of TAUC, compared to estimates from our model, as a function of survey density. The results (ADD FIGURE WITH EXPLANATORY CAPTION) show that TAUC is robust to survey density, but with unknown loss of precision. Finally, we examined how the TAUC in a year with few surveys compared to our model using repeated simulations, concluding (ADD FIGURE WITH EXPLANATORY CAPTION) that our model is more robust that TAUC in a year with few surveys. -->

## Adding covariates: biometrics and habitat indices




<!--# exactly how should biometrics and habitat data be included, and the value of that determined? -->

A wealth of information is collected as a part of spawner surveys, but not applied in analysis of spawner abundance. This typically includes length, sex, and age from otoliths or scales of dead spawners, likely augmented with biometrics from catches, counting fences, and dam passage. Relevant to the Osoyoos Lake Sockeye, @bailey2025 (their appendix table 5) determined that a large proportion of spawners were ocean age 1; in 2019: 55.8%, 2007: 48.2%, 2021: 41.9%, 2003: 38.3%. Those years were all low runs, \< 20,000. Assuming most age x.1 spawners are males (jacks), then the sex ratios are also anomalous, but note this population has an unusually high incidence of jills, age x.1 females (@major1962). Despite being so atypical, those four years are not outliers in @fig-timings.

## Multiple reaches, multiple rivers

<!--# Patrick: You might want to delete this subsection if it weakens your paper involving spatial parameters for spawner estimates.  I generalized to segments and river. Please improve, replace, or delete.  -->

This analysis of multiple years combined can be extended to spatial components. We foresee immediate application to predict (thus fit) the counts in individual segments of a spawning region, and conceivably further to include multiple rivers. This assumes spawning groups in those segments and rivers covary consistently as parts of one population. As an equation,

$$
N_{tjk} = N_j P_k \ \big(T_0[\mu,\sigma]_t + T_j[\mu,\sigma]_t + T_k[\mu,\sigma]_t \big) + \epsilon_{tjk}
$$

where $N_{tjk}$ is the number of spawners entering river *k* on day *t* in year *j*, estimated with error $\epsilon$ from the proportion $P_k$ of all spawners that year $N_j$ that enter river *k* according to how the background temporal pattern $T_0$ is affected by the pattern for that year $T_j$ and that river $T_k.$ Terms $T_x[\ ]_t$ are temporal distributions, with $\mu_x$ and $\sigma_x$ for timing and spread if Gaussian.

## Value of surveys for newly dead spawners

<!--# This has not been done in existing simulations. Do we want to do that now, and include it here? I vote this should be in another paper that actually uses the dead pitch information plus the live counts.  Go? No Go?  -->

If available, counts of newly dead spawners provide further information to estimate residence. Newly dead spawners accumulate between surveys, so the counts depend on survey intervals: the number of newly dead spawners is exits (deaths) cumulative to this survey day, minus deaths cumulative to the preceding survey. This assumes that dead spawners are marked by tags, by cutting off tails, or "dead-pitch" (removal from the river bank) to ensure they are only counted once. Some dead spawners will drift away, not counted, necessitating a factor to estimate fraction of dead observed.

The Okanagan River visual surveys of live spawners were completed in one day, and dead spawners were counted in those surveys (prior to 2000). Primarily for biometrics data and samples, dead-pitch surveys with durations of 3 to 5 days were also conducted about three times per year, and potentially overlapping the live surveys. Thus the live surveys observed dead spawners repeatedly but inconsistently. We intend a subsequent paper that adds the dead-pitch information and the inconsistent counts of dead spawners to our model.

<!--# see figure about dead spawners in Supplementary, below. Move here to explain why dead surveys differ from live surveys, or deleted.  -->

# Conclusions

This approach delivered estimates, with precisions, for annual values of:

1.  run size (spawner abundance), the key objective of spawner survey programs;
2.  arrival timing and arrival spread, parameters of ecological significance not determined by conventional analysis that present an opportunity to include more ecology in survey analyses.
3.  exit lag and exit spread, that replace the use of an assumed value for spawner residence time. We were able to back-calculate annual estimates of *effective residence* from TAUC/abundance.

<!--# do we want to reiterate the values for estimates, emphasizing precisions? -->

This model is reliable until survey density declines to gaps of 5 days or greater than 5 days. The analysis of multiple years at once will improve run size estimates in a year when few surveys were obtained.

# Recommendations

As a generalization, salmon datasets are not maintained following best practices ([@diack2024; @wilkinson2016]). As we discovered during this analysis, documenting survey methods, including data processing, is essential to avoid erroneous assumptions by analysts, such as our initial misinterpretation of counts of dead salmon. For examples, we point to the website [Monitoring Resources](MonitoringResources.org "MonitoringResources.org") maintained by the [**Pacific Northwest Aquatic Monitoring Partnership**](https://pnamp.org/)**.**

Improving the the accuracy of estimates of spawner run size appears possible by using more information, collected in, or relevant to, spawner surveys. With the caveat that adding complications and noisy datasets will have diminishing returns, we foresee including:

-   Habitat variables (river flow, temperature,) applied to improve the spawner entry models; likely asymmetric or discontinuous in response to the flow events characteristic of coastal salmon rivers. Temporal trends in run, timing, spread are valid covariates *per se*, but ideally related to causative habitat variables;

-   Covariates from the biometrics of spawners: age, sex, age\*sex, genomics, and genetics; essentially expanding the descriptions of salmon behaviour in our model.

-   Correlations between annual parameters and autocorrelation within parameters <!--# we have one example! # --> might improve accuracy; <!--# suggest method? # -->

-   Surveys in a year are autocorrelated, so each observation contributes less than one degree of freedom to a model, an important consideration; and

-   Sockeye spawners observed in specific year produce spawners returning three to five years later; the autocorrelation from this is not extraneous, but implies that spawner surveys are just one component integrated into analysis of data from the entire life-cycle.

    # Acknowledgements

Diligence, care, and persistence by survey field teams from the Okanagan Nations Alliance (ONA, [syilx.org](https://syilx.org/)) resulted in a spawners dataset based on unusually thorough and consistent methods; their commitment to *cause to come back* is inspiring. This paper commemorates accomplishments of the late Dr. Kim Hyatt who designed, implemented, and supported this survey program. We thank Karilyn Alex (ONA)  for access and permission regarding this proprietary data, possible through the data sharing agreement of the Canadian Okanagan Basin Technical Working Group (COBTWG). We thank Howard Stiff and Athena Ogden (Pacific Biological Station, Fisheries and Oceans Canada) and Elizabeth Ng (Four Peaks Environmental Ltd.) for the work required to reconcile and summarize 25 years of field notes and spreadsheets into the quality controlled and standardized dataset we received. Dr. James R. Irvine (DFO Pacific, retired) improved text based on his extensive knowledge of salmon surveys and related analyses.

<!--# deleted Collette Louie (Osoyoos Indian Band, [oib.ca](http://oib.ca/))I can’t find a reference to the data-sharing agreement *per se*.  -->

The authors declares no specific funding for this work.

## Data Availability

The data used in this study are the property of the Okanagan Nations Alliance. Please contact them directly concerning availability and acknowledgements: [Sylix.org](sylix.org).

# Figures {.unnumbered}

not used

# Tables {.unnumbered}

not used

# Supplementary Materials {.appendix}

```{=latex}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
```

Our selected model (m7), which uses a time-invariant $\mu^\text{exit lag}$, is specified as:\
$$ 
\begin{aligned} 
N_{y,d}^{\text{live}} &\sim \text{NegBinomial}(\mu^\text{live}_{y,d}, \phi^{\text{live}}) \\
\mu^\text{live}_{y,d} &=  \exp\left( \log\left(1 + 10^4 \times p^\text{arrived}_{y,d} \times (1 - p^\text{exited}_{y,d})\right) - \log(10^4) \right) \times N_y^{\text{spawners}} \\
p^\text{arrived}_{y,d} &= \Phi\left( \frac{ d - \mu^\text{arrival}_y }{ \sigma^\text{arrival}_y } \right) \\
p^\text{exited}_{y,d} &= \Phi\left( \frac{ d - (\mu^\text{arrival}_y + \mu^\text{exit lag})}{\sigma^\text{exit}_y} \right)\\
\log(N_y^{\text{spawners}}) &\sim \text{Normal}(10, 1)\\
\mu^\text{arrival}_y &\sim \text{Normal}(\mu^\text{arrival}_\mu, \mu^\text{arrival}_\sigma)\\
\mu^\text{arrival}_\mu &\sim \text{Normal}(40, 5)\\
\mu^\text{arrival}_\sigma &\sim \text{Exponential}(2)\\
\log(\sigma^\text{arrival}_y-1) &\sim \text{Normal}(\sigma^\text{arrival}_\mu, \sigma^\text{arrival}_\sigma)\\
\sigma^\text{arrival}_\mu &\sim \text{Normal}(\log(7), 0.2)\\
\sigma^\text{arrival}_\sigma &\sim \text{Exponential}(10)\\
\log(\mu^\text{exit lag}-1) &\sim \text{Normal}(\log(10), 0.3)\\
\log(\sigma^\text{exit}_y - 1) &\sim \text{Normal}(\sigma^\text{exit}_\mu, \sigma^\text{exit}_\sigma)\\
\sigma^\text{exit}_\mu &\sim \text{Normal}(\log(7), 0.2)\\
\sigma^\text{exit}_\sigma &\sim \text{Exponential}(10).\\
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-fullmodel}

```{r fig-obsfit, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.height=5, fig.width=8, fig.cap = "Observed survey counts versus predicted on linear (A) and log (B) scales. Points and error bars show the posterior median and the 66% and 95% credible intervals.",}
spread_draws(fit, live_mu[obs]) %>% 
  left_join(index_sk %>% mutate(obs = 1:n())) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = live, y = live_mu, group = obs))+
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0)+
  stat_pointinterval()+
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "k"), name = "Expected count (thousands)")+
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_number(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "k"), name = "Observed count (thousands)")+
  theme_bw()+
  
  spread_draws(fit, live_mu[obs]) %>% 
  left_join(index_sk %>% mutate(obs = 1:n())) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = live, y = live_mu, group = obs))+
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0)+
  stat_pointinterval()+
  scale_y_log10(name = "Expected count")+
  scale_x_log10(name = "Observed count")+
  theme_bw()+
  
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")
```

![Newly dead spawners each day (blue) is the rate of exits. Newly dead accumulate between surveys, here with alternating 1 day and 4 day separations, leading to an irregular pattern of counts (red). Parameters as in preceding figure.](../figures/newly_dead.png){#fig-newlydead fig-align="center" width="4in"}

The following documents are available for public access on [GitHub](https://github.com/SiRE-P/Multilevel-Spawners) and [Zenodo](https://support.zenodo.org/help/en-gb): FIX LINKS

1.  spawners.stan – Stan code for the models

2.  Spawners_est.R – This is the R code to run the Stan model and plot results.

3.  Newly Dead Spawners.pdf – Simulations report as PDF.

4.  Newly Dead Spawners.qmd – Simulation code to describe, run, and report the model.

5.  newly dead spawners.stan – Stan code for simulations.

# References {.unnumbered}
